comments.body: Evidence for AC 'make lint && make typecheck && make test pass': verified locally — make lint clean (ruff+format+zizmor+actionlint), make typecheck clean (mypy 327 files), full suite green. Also by CI: Gerrit change 717 (https://rebar.solutions.navateam.com/c/rebar/+/717) carries Verified+1 (CI build/test/lint/typecheck on GitHub Actions across py3.11/3.12/3.13 + macOS).

compiled_state.description:
# S3 — OIDC / JWKS JWT verifier

## Why
JWKS-backed JWT validation is the "OAuth" half of the confirmed floor and the generic "plug into
any OIDC provider" path — one verifier covers AWS Cognito, Microsoft Entra ID, Auth0, Okta,
Keycloak, WorkOS, Google ID tokens, and GitHub Actions OIDC (all publish a JWKS and issue standard
JWTs). It is the highest-leverage verifier for the "wide variety of environments" goal.

## What
Add a **`JWKSTokenVerifier`** to `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py`, selected by the `jwt` token in
`mcp.auth_strategies`, with flat config keys it registers: `auth_jwt_jwks_uri`, `auth_jwt_issuer`,
`auth_jwt_algorithms` (default `RS256,ES256`), `auth_jwt_leeway` (seconds, default 60),
`auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown` (seconds, default 30), `auth_jwt_jwks_timeout` (seconds, default
10), `auth_jwt_expected_typ` (default **unset**), and `auth_jwt_allow_private_jwks_host` (bool,
default `false`). It does **not** take a per-verifier audience — it validates
`aud == auth_resource_server_url` (the single resource from S2). Implemented with PyJWT
(`jwt.PyJWKClient` + `jwt.decode`); add `pyjwt[crypto]` to the `[mcp]` extra (the SDK pin lives in
S1). It validates the signature (pinned algorithms), `iss`, `aud`, `exp`/`iat`/`nbf`, optionally the
header `typ` (when `auth_jwt_expected_typ` is set — see Security), maps `scope`/`scp`→scopes, sets
`expires_at` from `exp` (a real positive value), and stamps `resource=auth_resource_server_url` so
the composite audience check passes. This story registers its keys in `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json` +
regenerates the reference docs.

## Approach & rationale
Use PyJWT's `PyJWKClient` (JWKS fetch + in-process key cache + `kid` lookup) and `jwt.decode` with an
explicit pinned `algorithms` allowlist — PyJWT's documented JWKS path and the same shape FastMCP's
`JWTVerifier` uses, giving provider-generic OIDC validation in a small module on the SDK seam. PyJWT
is already a transitive of `mcp`; `pyjwt[crypto]` makes the `cryptography` backend explicit for RS/ES
verification. **PyJWKClient's `lifespan` is a cache TTL, not an unknown-`kid` flood guard**, so this
verifier implements the unknown-`kid` refetch cooldown itself (see the concurrency-safe design in
Security) rather than relying on the library for it.

**Async-safety of the blocking JWKS fetch.** `PyJWKClient.get_signing_key_from_jwt()` (and its
underlying JWKS HTTP fetch) is **synchronous/blocking**, but the verifier's `verify_token` runs on
FastMCP's async event loop. The verifier therefore offloads every key lookup / JWKS fetch to a worker
thread via `asyncio.to_thread(...)` so it never blocks the event loop, and it serializes the
cooldown check-and-update with an `asyncio.Lock` (see Security) so the "at most one fetch per
cooldown window" guarantee holds under concurrent requests.

## Scope
The JWKS/JWT verifier only, on the S2 seam. Not introspection/proxy/custom. Subset of the epic.
Depends on S2.

## Security
Boundary-crossing. Hard requirements (RFC 8725 JWT BCP):
- **Algorithm pinning:** reject `alg:none`; never derive the algorithm from the token header. Also
  **refuse to start** if any **symmetric** algorithm is configured in `auth_jwt_algorithms` together
  with a JWKS source (asymmetric-only on the JWKS path) — this blocks the RS256↔HS256 confusion
  attack (signing HS256 with the public key as the HMAC secret). The symmetric set that triggers the
  startup refusal is the closed, explicitly-enumerated family **`{HS256, HS384, HS512}`** (case-
  insensitive); anything outside the asymmetric families `RS*/PS*/ES*/EdDSA` on a JWKS source is
  likewise refused.
- Mandatory `aud == auth_resource_server_url` (RFC 8707), `iss == auth_jwt_issuer`, and `exp`/`nbf`
  with bounded `leeway`.
- **Cross-JWT-confusion `typ` check (configurable, because OIDC providers vary):** when
  `auth_jwt_expected_typ` is set, the JWT header `typ` MUST equal it (RFC 8725 §3.11 — e.g.
  `at+JWT` per the RFC 9068 access-token profile), so an ID token or other same-issuer/same-aud JWT
  cannot be replayed as an access token. It defaults **unset** (check skipped, relying on
  `aud`+`iss`+`scope`) because many IdPs do not stamp `typ` on access tokens; S7 documents that
  operators SHOULD set `auth_jwt_expected_typ=at+JWT` for RFC 9068-compliant IdPs.
- **SSRF-safe JWKS fetch:** `jwks_uri` is fixed at construction from trusted config (never the token
  — no `jku`/`x5u`), **HTTPS-only** (an `http://` URI is rejected at construction), with a bounded
  HTTP timeout given by the named key **`auth_jwt_jwks_timeout` (seconds, default `10`)**, passed as
  the `timeout=` argument to `PyJWKClient` so a slow/hung JWKS endpoint cannot stall the verifier.
  Private/link-local hosts (e.g. `169.254.169.254`, RFC 1918, loopback) are **rejected by default**;
  an operator with a legitimate internal IdP sets `auth_jwt_allow_private_jwks_host=true` to opt out.
- **Key rotation without DoS (concurrency-safe):** the JWKS is cached with a bounded TTL; the verifier
  caps refetch on an unknown `kid` to **at most one refetch per `auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown`
  seconds (default 30)** (implemented by the verifier itself, not PyJWKClient's `lifespan`). Because
  `verify_token` runs concurrently on the async event loop, the cooldown's read-check-then-update of
  the last-refetch timestamp is a check-then-act (TOCTOU) hazard; it is therefore made **atomic under
  an `asyncio.Lock`** that serializes the check-and-update, so two coroutines seeing the same
  unknown `kid` cannot both trigger a refetch. Result: an attacker flooding random `kid`s cannot force
  more than one JWKS fetch per cooldown window even under concurrency, while a legitimate rotation
   still refetches after the cooldown.
  **Timestamp-on-failure policy:** the last-refetch timestamp is updated on every fetch *attempt* (success OR failure), not only on success, so a JWKS endpoint that errors cannot be used to bypass the flood guard by making fetches fail. The cost is bounded: a transient JWKS outage delays a legitimate refetch by at most one `auth_jwt_jwks_refetch_cooldown` window (default 30 s), and during that window a fetch failure raises → the composite denies (the request fails regardless), so no invalid token is admitted; operators seeing persistent outages lengthen the endpoint's availability, not this guard.
- A JWKS-fetch failure raises (composite treats as non-acceptance → deny); a definitively invalid
  token returns None. All verify-path errors go through the S2 redaction helper so no token/secret
  substring is ever logged.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `pyproject.toml` adds `pyjwt[crypto]` to `[mcp]`; `make typecheck` resolves the imports.
- [ ] The `auth_jwt_*` flat keys (including `auth_jwt_jwks_timeout`) are registered in
      `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`server.json`, appear in the regenerated reference docs, and
      `scripts/check_server_manifest.py` passes.
- [ ] Unit test (tier: unit against the real verifier; the irreducible boundary — the JWKS HTTP
      fetch — is a stateful fake serving a locally-generated key set) asserts: a JWT with correct
      `iss`/`aud`(=resource) → an `AccessToken` with the expected scopes AND
      `resource==auth_resource_server_url` and an `expires_at` **equal to the token's `exp` claim**
      (observable oracle: returned `AccessToken.expires_at` == the exact `exp` used to mint the JWT,
      not merely a positive value).
- [ ] Unit tests assert rejection for: wrong `iss`; `aud` ≠ `auth_resource_server_url`; expired
      `exp`; `nbf` in the future; `alg:none`; and an `HS256` token signed with the RSA public key as
      the HMAC secret — each a distinct negative control.
- [ ] Unit test asserts that with `auth_jwt_expected_typ=at+JWT` set, a token whose header `typ` is
      absent/mismatched is rejected while a matching one is accepted (observable oracle: accept vs
      reject); and that with `auth_jwt_expected_typ` unset the typ check is skipped.
- [ ] Unit test asserts the verifier **refuses to start** when `auth_jwt_algorithms` includes a
      symmetric algorithm (`HS256`/`HS384`/`HS512`) alongside a JWKS source (observable oracle:
      startup raises).
- [ ] Unit test asserts construction-time SSRF guards: an `http://` `jwks_uri` is rejected; a
      private/link-local host is rejected by default and accepted only with
      `auth_jwt_allow_private_jwks_host=true`; and the JWKS client is constructed with
      `timeout=auth_jwt_jwks_timeout` (default `10` s) (observable oracle: for the SSRF rejections,
      construction raises; for the timeout, the test patches `jwt.PyJWKClient` and asserts it was
      called with keyword `timeout=10` — the concrete default value — so the assertion is exact and
      unambiguous, not merely 'a timeout is passed').
- [ ] Unit test asserts a JWKS **fetch failure raises** (→ composite non-acceptance/deny), distinct
      from a definitively-invalid token which returns None.
- [ ] Unit test asserts unknown-`kid` cooldown is concurrency-safe: a burst of concurrent requests
      with distinct unknown `kid`s within one cooldown window triggers **exactly one** JWKS fetch
      (observable oracle: the stateful JWKS fake's request count == 1 for the burst — not 0, which
      would mean the lock wrongly blocks all fetches, and not >1, which would mean the TOCTOU
      double-fetch was not prevented), while a token signed by
      a rotated key validates after the cooldown elapses (a legitimate rotation still refetches).
- [ ] Integration test (httpx `ASGITransport`) asserts a valid JWKS-issued token → 200 and a
      garbage bearer → 401 end-to-end through the transport.
- [ ] `make lint && make typecheck && make test` pass.

## Compatibility
Additive verifier, opt-in via the `jwt` strategy. The `pyjwt[crypto]` add is backward-compatible;
the SDK pin already lives in S1.

file_impact.reason: new: JWT validation + alg-confusion + rotation tests
